articles.headline: William Yates, Esq.: 'Slavery and Colored People in Delaware' (Concluded)
articles.author: William Yates, Esq.
articles.summary: Conclusion of Yates's investigative report on Delaware. Argues Delaware's discriminatory laws are self-defeating (driving away capital and skilled Black labor it depends on), quotes Delaware's 1767 manumission-law preamble condemning free Black people as 'idle and slothful,' and profiles successful free Black Delawareans as counter-evidence: two Francisco brothers who farmed as tenants and left for Ohio with $10,000 in savings; a Black mechanic who built half the houses in Camden and whose son, also a mason, has $1,000 credit; and the builder of Dover's largest hotel. Also reprints a companion letter 'Ministerial Neutrality' (signed 'T.', from the Herald of Freedom) rebuking clergy who decline to take an anti-slavery stand from the pulpit.

articles.headline: A Colored Baptist Preacher at Nassau, Bahamas
articles.type: reprint (from a London-published narrative by a Mr. Sweeney)
articles.summary: Reprints a British traveler's account of meeting Frank Spence, a Black Baptist minister born enslaved in the southern United States who came to Nassau, New Providence as a youth. Spence built his own meeting-house by hand and with his own earnings (estimated at over £1,000 currency), and used his savings to purchase freedom for his wife ($300) and each of his children ($100 each). He preached three times each Sunday plus a weekday service and held daily 6am prayer meetings so his congregation could attend before work.
articles.notes: Corroborates the paper's recurring argument that free/self-emancipated Black people are capable of institution-building; useful comparison point to the Philadelphia 'Facts for the People' statistical series in the same issue's page 4.

articles.headline: Thomas Van Rensalaer's reply in the H. Pattinson Temperance House affair
articles.author: Thomas Van Rensalaer (letter); editorial response by Samuel E. Cornish
articles.summary: Follow-up to the paper's exposé of Cornish being refused tea at H. Pattinson's Temperance House on account of color (reported in the Aug. 12 issue). Van Rensalaer writes to clear himself of the charge that he instigated the attack on Pattinson's business, stating he never discussed Pattinson with the editors before the article ran. A separate correspondent's letter recounts a slaveholding 'Squire Smith' who candidly told a friend he lets his slaves 'shift for themselves' once 'old and useless,' and that the friend's gentle remonstrance visibly struck Smith's conscience. The editors accept Van Rensalaer's account and print it 'as the fact in the case.'

articles.headline: 'Prejudice Among Ourselves' -- anti-colorism essay
articles.author: signed 'A Colored American'
articles.summary: Argues against prejudice based on shade of complexion among Black people themselves, urging unity: 'That colored man who is prejudiced against another colored man on account of color, should never complain of the prejudices of the whites.' Praises the paper's chosen name 'Colored American' as a unifying term embracing all complexions in place of divisive labels like 'blacks and mulattoes,' which the writer says only serves the interests of those who wish to 'keep the colored people distracted and divided among themselves.' Dated Boston, August 1837; signed 'Your brother, John [illegible surname]' in the body, with the byline 'A Colored American.'

articles.headline: 'Another Good Example' -- Frederick A. Hinton and Sabbath observance
articles.author: unsigned correspondent, Philadelphia
articles.summary: Reports learning in Philadelphia that Frederick A. Hinton, hairdresser, has for a year now kept his shop closed on the Sabbath, following the earlier example of agent John B. Vashon of Pittsburgh (previously noted in the paper). The writer proposes to publish the names of other Black tradesmen, especially hairdressers, who adopt the same practice, to encourage more to 'Remember the Sabbath day, to keep it holy.'

articles.headline: New York suffrage-petition organizing letter
articles.author: signed 'Thomas Van Rensalaer' (per byline at close of a related letter on the same page)
articles.summary: Recounts New York City's suffrage petition campaigns to date -- roughly 200-300 signatures in Feb./March 1834, about 620 in the following year, plus companion petitions from Brooklyn, Albany, Troy, Oswego and Genesee counties -- and estimates that of an estimated ~300 Black New Yorkers holding the $250 freehold property required to vote, only about 50 are registered and perhaps 10 actually vote in a given year. Calls for a renewed, better-organized, simultaneous statewide petition campaign to the state legislature.

articles.headline: Public meeting on Black disenfranchisement -- officers and call to petition
articles.author: T. S. Sidney and Samuel Enals (signers); James Forten Sr. presiding
articles.summary: A circular addressed 'To the Colored Citizens of the State of [Pennsylvania]' (page badly OCR-degraded) reporting a public meeting organized to oppose the pending constitutional disenfranchisement of Black voters, with James Forten Sr. as President, Jacob C. White as Vice President, and James Forten Jr. and William Whipper as Secretaries; calls for petitions to the state legislature at its next session. Dated New York, August 1, 1837, and signed by Thomas S. Sidney and Samuel Enals -- the same New York committeemen active in the Phoenix Hall suffrage-organizing meetings reported in the Aug. 5/12 issues -- indicating New York activists' solidarity with the Philadelphia-centered fight against Pennsylvania's 1837-38 disenfranchisement convention.

articles.headline: 'Facts for the People,' Nos. 1-8: statistical portrait of Black Philadelphia
articles.author: signed 'C.'
articles.summary: Continues a monthly statistical series rebutting claims that Black Philadelphians are a public burden: colored paupers relieved by the Guardians of the Poor are ~4% of the total despite being ~8.5% of the city's population; the city's Black community pays roughly $500/year more in taxes than it draws in poor relief; supports 55 Benevolent Societies (some incorporated) spending $10,000/year in mutual aid, ensuring no Black pauper is buried at public expense; maintains 15 churches (worth $172,000), 34 ministers, 17 Sabbath schools, a public library (~800 volumes), literary, Bible, moral-reform and temperance societies; pays ~$18,000/year rent and owns real estate worth $600,000 plus $226,000 in personal property among roughly two-thirds of the population surveyed; and reports 400-500 Black tradesmen/mechanics in the city and suburbs despite apprenticeship prejudice.

articles.headline: Endorsement of the Colored American by Theodore S. Wright and Gerrit Smith; Whipper's matching-fund pledge
articles.summary: Rev. Theodore S. Wright and Gerrit Smith sign a printed endorsement recommending the Colored American, edited by Rev. S. E. Cornish, to abolitionists and especially to Black readers, citing confidence in the editor's abilities. On the same page, William Whipper's earlier pledge to be 'ONE OF A HUNDRED' subscribers giving $10 each toward the editor's salary is renewed with a call for others to match it.

articles.headline: Edward V. Clark succeeds Charles B. Ray as bootmaker
articles.summary: Advertisement for Edward V. Clark, 'successor to Mr. Charles B. Ray,' Boot and Shoemaker on Pearl Street, New York -- documenting that General Agent Rev. Charles B. Ray's original trade before his ministry/agency work was bootmaking, a business now carried on by Edward V. Clark (who appears elsewhere in this issue and the Sept. 2 issue as an officer of New York's new Young Men's Literary and Moral Reform Society).

articles.headline: On the constitutionality of Congress abolishing the slave trade and slavery in the District of Columbia
articles.summary: Heavily degraded OCR, but the essay argues at length for Congress's exclusive constitutional authority over the District of Columbia and therefore its power to abolish both the domestic slave trade and slavery there, reviewing precedents including an 1816 act restricting slave importation into the District. Notes President Martin Van Buren's pledge, 'out of respect to the wishes of slaveholders,' not to sign any bill abolishing slavery in the District.

articles.headline: Planning for a Moral Reform Convention -- Philadelphia's leading role
articles.summary: Discusses plans (heavily OCR-degraded) for a Moral Reform Convention to bring together delegates from colored citizens in various states, with the writer arguing Philadelphia's 'brethren' occupy the most favorable position to lead the cause of moral reform given the city's institutional strength. Names William Whipper and James Forten in connection with the effort.

articles.headline: 'Doctrines of Abolitionists' -- catechism (installment)
articles.summary: A numbered catechism of abolitionist doctrine (paralleling the American Anti-Slavery Society declaration reprinted in the June 17 issue): (1) slavery is a sin and its perpetuation can never be politically right; (2) enslavers, not those who brought Africans to America generations ago, bear the guilt; (3) American slavery is a legalized wrong against both God and the enslaved; (4) the Bible requires immediate repentance of sin, so slaveholders should immediately cease holding slaves; (5) all people are born free, so the status of 'slave' is itself illegitimate regardless of law. Answers the recurring charge that abolitionists secretly favor 'amalgamation' (racial intermarriage) by denying the charge and turning it back on slaveholders, citing the widespread mixed-race enslaved population as evidence of exploitation by slaveholders themselves.

articles.headline: 'Important Meeting' -- Public meeting of colored young men of New York City, Aug. 21 1837
articles.summary: Minutes of 'A Public Meeting of the Colored young men of the city of New York,' held August 21, 1837 at Philomathean Hall (corrected date/detail from a cleaner Google-Drive-indexed OCR pass of the source PDF, which reads noticeably better than this run's tesseract pass on several key names). Called to order by Edward V. Clark; Henry Seaman elected President; Edward V. Clark, Henry Highland Garnet, and Charles L. Reason elected Vice Presidents; J. Zuille among the Secretaries. On motion of Peter P. Simons, constitutional/procedural business was handled; on motion of Thomas S. Riley, a resolution pledged mutual support among 'the colored young men of the State' and called for simultaneous, organized petitioning of the Legislature; a further resolution (moved, per this run's own OCR pass, by a 'Daniel Elston') declared laws depriving colored citizens of suffrage 'wholly unconstitutional.' The meeting explicitly invokes Black New Yorkers' War of 1812 militia service on the northern frontier as grounds for their claim to full citizenship rights.
articles.notes: This appears to be the SAME founding meeting referenced by the adjacent society-constitution text on this page (see next entry), not a separate rival body -- both entries should probably collapse into one event; kept partly separate here because the page layout/OCR made the boundary between 'meeting minutes' and 'society constitution' text hard to pin down with certainty. Recommend a future pass re-reading the original PDF page image to confirm.

articles.headline: Constitution and founders' roster of a Literary and Moral Reform Society (New York-area, possibly Newburgh)
articles.author: letter signed John N. Templeton (Corresponding Secretary)
articles.summary: Full constitution of a new young men's Literary and Moral Reform Society, styled auxiliary to the American Moral Reform Society: membership open to men of good habits and respectability aged 18-35 on payment of 50 cents entrance and 12.5 cents monthly dues; governed by a President, Vice President, two Secretaries (Corresponding and Recording), and seven Counselors forming a Board of Managers; annual meeting the second Monday in May; monthly meetings the first Friday. A 'Founders of the Young Men's [Society]' list (corrected via the cleaner Drive-indexed OCR) names: Turner Newman, Wm. J. Grandy, Martin Delany, Orange Lewis, Matthew Jones, Edward Parker, Vincent A. Jolly, George Barker (or Parker -- OCR ambiguous), John N. Templeton, and John H. Butler. A cover letter (signed John N. Templeton, Corresponding Secretary) reports the society is new and small ('number at present only sixteen') and still building its library, but hopes to grow, and thanks the paper for giving voice to 'a people here [who] have life, liberty ... and a paper edited by a colored man.'
articles.notes: FLAG FOR MICHIKO: one of the ten founders is transcribed (in the cleaner Drive OCR pass) as 'Martin Delany' -- possibly Martin R. Delany (b. 1812), who would have been ~24-25 in 1837. His documented 1837 base was Pittsburgh (he co-founded Pittsburgh's Theban Literary Society c.1832-33 and did not launch The Mystery until 1843), so a Hudson Valley/NYC-area founding membership this early would be a notable addition to his biography if confirmed -- but this could equally be a different, unrelated Martin Delany. Recommend checking the original PDF image directly before treating this as a confirmed Delany appearance; loaded into the DB as a plain 'newspaper'-source person for now rather than matched to any existing Delany record.

articles.headline: General Agent Charles B. Ray's travel letter -- Troy, Newburgh, Poughkeepsie
articles.author: Rev. Charles B. Ray
articles.summary: Ray (dateline Troy, N.Y., Aug. 10, 1837) describes departing New York by the steamer Champlin, stopping at Newburgh and Poughkeepsie to organize suffrage-petition committees, then arriving in Troy. Describes a Monday evening meeting of Troy's Black community at a Presbyterian church that went well, followed by a Tuesday evening meeting at the Seaman's Bethel where Ray addressed a mixed-race audience for an hour and ten minutes on the condition of Black Americans; reports a favorable impression made on the white attendees. Also describes a Wednesday morning walk to a hill overlooking Troy and the U.S. Arsenal with friends including 'Van Rens[selaer?]' (name partly illegible).
articles.notes: Embedded in or immediately adjacent to this letter (attribution to Ray uncertain due to OCR page-layout confusion) is a vivid first-person dialogue recounting being refused tea in a steamboat's main cabin and directed to eat with the deck hands in the kitchen -- the narrator declines to eat 'from necessity' this time and presses the point of principle with the boat's officers, who repeatedly defer responsibility up the chain of command ('the owner of the boat says...'). See separate article entry.

articles.headline: Steamboat cabin discrimination -- 'we are willing to wait, and eat at the second table'
articles.author: unsigned first-person narrator (likely embedded in or appended to C. B. Ray's travel letter on the same page)
articles.summary: A Black traveler and companion, aboard a steamboat, are told at teatime that they cannot be served in the cabin because 'all our deck hands eat in the kitchen, and all persons of color, generally... this is the general rule.' The narrator offers to wait and eat at a 'second table' rather than accept exclusion outright; successive crew members (a steward, then someone 'acting as captain') each defer the decision further up the chain -- 'the captain is not on board... you can see what he will say' -- ending without full resolution in the excerpt as printed. A close parallel to the Samuel Cornish/Pattinson's Temperance House incident reported two issues earlier.

articles.headline: William Appo advertisement/notice
articles.summary: A notice for William Appo (partial OCR: 'Wm. Appo, 15[2/3] Church St.') -- likely the Philadelphia-connected Black musician of the well-known Appo family (Joseph and William Appo played in the mixed Kemble orchestra at the Walnut St. Theatre in 1826, per the Pencil Pusher batch of extractions). Flag for merge-candidate review against the existing Winch/Pencil-Pusher Appo record(s).

articles.headline: Subscriber and agent correspondence -- George Cary (Cincinnati) and a Palmyra Quaker reader
articles.author: George Cary; unnamed Palmyra, NY correspondent
articles.summary: George Cary, the paper's Cincinnati, Ohio agent, writes with subscription business and general encouragement for the paper's cause. Separately, a Quaker correspondent from Palmyra, NY (dated in Quaker month-numeral style, '6th month') encloses $10 for six copies of the paper, one intended for 'Mr. [name garbled], (Colored) man of our village of much promin[ence]... who takes great interest in the success of thy paper.'

articles.headline: 'Amalgamation -- Equal Privileges' (concluded) and a rescue involving Robert Purvis
articles.summary: Concludes a series rebutting the charge that abolitionists secretly favor interracial 'amalgamation,' arguing the charge is hypocritical given how common forced sexual exploitation of enslaved women by enslavers already is, evidenced by the mixed-race complexions common among the enslaved population under partus sequitur ventrem. Embedded in the same column is a brief, garbled anecdote describing a tense public confrontation (jeers, a horse and carriage, a crowd escorting someone to the edge of a village) in which 'the friendly hand of R. Purvis... immediately relieved him from this embarrassment' -- likely Robert Purvis intervening to protect or assist someone facing a hostile crowd, possibly connected to the fugitive-slave case reported immediately below in the same issue.

articles.headline: Fugitive slave case in Bucks County, PA (Doylestown)
articles.author: reprinted from the National Inquirer
articles.summary: Reports a habeas corpus hearing at Doylestown, Bucks County, PA (dateline 'Doylestown, [Aug.] 10, 1837') before Judge Fox concerning a Maryland claimant's attempt to reclaim an alleged fugitive under Maryland slave law. The claimant's attorney argued for recognizing the Maryland statute; the court overruled the offered proof, finding the copy of the Maryland digest submitted was not properly authenticated and so could not be received as evidence in a Pennsylvania court, on the same principle that a Pennsylvania law digest could not be self-authenticating evidence in a Maryland court. The claimant's counsel then sought a continuance, which was contested.

events.name: Public Meeting of the Colored Young Men of the City of New York
events.description: New York City's Black young men hold a public meeting combining self-improvement (a Literary and Moral Reform Society, library) with active suffrage-petition organizing, explicitly invoking Black New Yorkers' War of 1812 militia service as grounds for citizenship claims, and calling on young Black men of the Hudson River towns and Long Island to organize parallel petitions to the state legislature against Pennsylvania-style disenfranchisement.
attendees: called meeting to order; Vice President
moved a resolution on unconstitutionality of disenfranchisement (per this run's own OCR pass)

events.name: Founding of a Literary and Moral Reform Society (New York-area)
events.location: New York State (Newburgh area; exact town uncertain)
events.description: A new young men's society (18-35 years old, 50-cent entrance, 12.5-cent monthly dues) organized as an auxiliary of the American Moral Reform Society, with a constitution establishing a President, Vice President, two Secretaries, and a seven-member Board of Counselors; annual meeting each May, monthly meetings the first Friday. Membership only sixteen at time of writing.
attendees: founding member -- possibly Martin R. Delany; UNCONFIRMED, flagged for review

events.name: Fugitive slave habeas hearing, Doylestown (Bucks County), PA
events.description: Habeas corpus hearing before Judge Fox on a Maryland claimant's attempt to recover an alleged fugitive slave; the court ruled the claimant's proof of Maryland slave law inadmissible for lack of proper authentication of the statute-book copy offered. Robert Purvis appears to have assisted the accused/community in a related public confrontation described in the same issue.
